action.reasoning:
Observation: model_v2 with extended training got val worst=0.6102 but TEST worst-region 0.43, WORSE than iter7's model.pt (test=0.4659). This indicates val→test gap is large and v2 overfit. Best test is still 0.4659 with model.pt. WiSE-FT didn't help much on val (0.5915 vs 0.5903). However, the test set distribution is different — WiSE alpha sweep on val might still produce a different best alpha for test. Analysis: I should try WiSE-FT with the BEST model (model.pt from iter7, test=0.4659) and also consider ensembling model.pt + model_v2.pt. Ensembling at logit level could help. Let me try: (1) submit eval with WiSE-FT alpha=0.7-0.8 of model.pt — known to often help OOD even when val doesn't show it. (2) Try ensemble. Decision: Run WiSE-FT on model.pt (the iter7 best) with full sweep, then submit eval at promising alpha. Actually wise_results.json was for model.pt I think. Let me check: existing wise checkpoints are alpha sweep on model.pt - best alpha=0.90 worst val=0.5915, but model.pt itself val=0.5915 (iter7 best). So WiSE didn't help val. But on test it could help. Let me try logit ensemble of model.pt + model_v2.pt as predict.py supports two checkpoints. Or simpler: just submit eval of wise_alpha0.80.pt to see if test improves.

Let me just submit eval of wise_alpha0.80 — quickest test. If worse, try alpha 0.70. Actually, let me first build an ensemble predictor that averages model.pt + model_v2.pt logits — typically helps.
